---
title: "Stable Capability Fields"
chapter_id: "stable-capability-fields"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "scf"
  - "viea"
  - "talos"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Stable Capability Fields

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `stable-capability-fields` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `scf`; supporting: `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`, `moecot`; raw cache: `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats Stable Capability Fields as a governed substitution boundary. It does not claim that SCF safety properties are implemented here beyond the schema fixture and any explicitly built proofs.

## Problem

The stack needs stable semantic boundaries so implementations can improve without changing what a capability means.

The previous chapter makes rights technical. This chapter gives the system a way to improve components without moving the target under those rights. A capability must have a stable meaning before it can be routed to, qualified, replaced, rolled back, or governed. Otherwise every implementation change is also a semantic change, and no one can tell whether the system improved or merely redefined success.

SCF supplies the core distinction: a field is the durable semantic boundary; an implementation is a replaceable realization of that boundary. The field carries identity, interface, authority ceiling, evaluator policy, qualification context, route validity, state-migration obligations, evidence, lifecycle state, and recovery paths. That separation is what lets recursive improvement be bounded instead of ambient.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Plugin replacement and model swapping do not define identity, authority, qualification evidence, or rollback semantics.

Replacing a model, tool, prompt, adapter, or specialist can look like an ordinary upgrade while changing the actual capability. A faster implementation may drop edge cases. A stronger model may gain broader authority through a route that was only meant for a narrow field. A new evaluator may be easier to satisfy. A migration may preserve outputs while corrupting hidden state. None of these failures is visible if the system treats "use the new one" as the whole upgrade protocol.

SCF makes the upgrade question testable: does this candidate satisfy the same field contract, under this qualification context, without expanding authority, losing regressions, capturing the evaluator, or destroying rollback?

## Core Claim

[stable-capability-fields.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] A stable capability field is a governed boundary with identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, and rollback policy.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The SCF source supplies the field/implementation/evaluator vocabulary; VIEA and Talos supply artifact and execution context; Ladon/Manhattan supplies authority-handle pressure. The chapter does not treat the source's reported executable fragments as locally verified.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Stable Capability Field record.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Stable field identity"] --> B["Interface contract"]
  B --> C["Authority ceiling"]
  C --> D["Candidate implementation"]
  D --> E{"Qualification context"}
  E --> F{"Evaluator integrity"}
  F --> G{"Route validity"}
  G -- "pass" --> H["Lifecycle: canary / qualified / default"]
  G -- "fail" --> I["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
  H --> J["Evidence + regression ledger"]
  I --> J
  J --> K["Rollback and recovery path"]
```

- Separate field identity from implementation.
- Bind qualification claims to exact artifacts.
- Represent lifecycle transitions such as shadow, canary, qualified, default, deprecated, and retired.
- Require route validity and evaluator integrity before promotion.

Qualification is scoped, not global. A candidate can be qualified for one domain, risk tier, authority ceiling, hardware context, benchmark epoch, or user population without being qualified everywhere. Its evidence should be append-only enough that later readers can reconstruct what was known when it was promoted.

The route proposer may be broad and heuristic; the route validator must be narrow and explicit. It checks field identity, claims, leases, profiles, grants, state paths, composition certificates, and authority ceilings before the candidate can become the default route.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Stable Capability Field record.

- Planning requests field capabilities.
- Execution invokes authorized routes.
- Evidence records qualification and regression results.

Minimum fields:

- `field_id`
- `semantic_boundary`
- `interface_contract`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `implementations`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `qualification_context`
- `qualification_predicates`
- `evaluator_policy`
- `route_validity`
- `evidence_refs`
- `state_migration`
- `regression_suite`
- `rollback_plan`

Planning requests a field by semantic boundary and capability profile. Execution invokes only authorized routes. Evidence records qualification, regressions, incidents, appeals, and lifecycle transitions. Governance decides whether a field can broaden authority or change evaluator policy.

## Invariants

- Replacement cannot expand authority by default.
- Evaluator integrity is protected.
- Qualification context is explicit and time-bound.
- Rollback remains available after failed mutation.

The strongest invariant is identity binding: replacing an implementation must not change the field's semantic boundary unless governance explicitly creates a new field or version. Authority is also bound to the field, not inherited from the raw power of the candidate implementation.

## Failure modes

- Field identity drift.
- Evaluator capture.
- Authority expansion during replacement.

Field identity drift happens when the name stays stable while the behavior changes. Evaluator capture happens when the candidate influences the policy or measurement that qualifies it. Authority expansion happens when a more capable implementation inherits a route that was safe only for a weaker one. Each should block default promotion until a replacement transaction records the residual and review path.

## Minimal implementation

The smallest useful implementation is the existing `stable_capability_field` schema expanded with lifecycle, qualification context, evaluator policy, route validity, evidence references, state migration, and rollback metadata. Passing schema validation only proves the record is shaped correctly; it does not prove a candidate is qualified.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Stable capability field fixture validation | Check that the SCF fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Qualification predicate test | Check that a candidate cannot enter `qualified` without satisfying the field's declared predicates in context. | planned; not run |
| Route validity test | Check that route grants, profile claims, leases, and state paths are valid for the field. | planned; not run |
| Authority non-escalation test | Check that candidate power does not expand the field authority ceiling by default. | planned; not run |
| Rollback readiness test | Check that recovery and state-migration paths exist before default promotion. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:scf.field_identity.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields` | An implementation can replace another only if it satisfies the field qualification predicate. | planned |
| `lean:scf.field_identity.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields` | A replacement that expands authority without a governance grant is rejected. | planned |

These proof targets remain planned. The existing schema is the executable boundary today; promotion and authority properties still need tests or Lean predicates.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk identifies the sources for field identity and lifecycle vocabulary. It does not promote SCF safety claims without local artifacts or exact source-to-claim mapping.

## Summary

Stable Capability Fields are the stack's upgrade sockets. They let a capability improve by changing implementations while preserving semantic identity, authority ceilings, evaluator policy, evidence, route validity, and rollback.

This chapter makes self-improvement possible without making every upgrade a constitutional event. The next chapter turns field updates into explicit replacement transactions.
